title: Khoresh fesenjan
subtitle: Persian pomegranate-walnut chicken stew
excerpt: Khoresh fesenjan is one of the great Persian stews: chicken browned and then slow-cooked in a sauce of finely ground walnuts and pomegranate syrup until the sauce turns a deep, almost black brown and the chicken is falling-tender. Sweet, sour, and rich in one bowl.
sourceNotes:
• Margaret Shaida, *The Legendary Cuisine of Persia* (1992). The principal authority for the walnut-to-pomegranate ratio and the slow-reduction technique.
• Claudia Roden, *A New Book of Middle Eastern Food* (1985). Cross-reference for the Persian khoresh canon and the sourness calibration with pomegranate syrup.
• Najmieh Batmanglij, *Food of Life: Ancient Persian and Modern Iranian Cooking* (2011). Used for the spicing approach and the serving convention with saffron rice.

content.text: There is a point in cooking
khoresh
fesenjan

when the walnut sauce turns from pale brown to a colour close to dark chocolate. That is when the flavour locks in: the bitterness of the walnut softens, the pomegranate syrup loses its sharp edge, and the two pull together into something rich and almost savoury-sweet. It is one of the more transformative things that happens in a pot.

The dish comes from the north of Iran, and versions appear at celebratory meals alongside saffron rice and herb frittata. At home, it is less formal but no less good. The method is not complicated: brown the chicken, cook down the walnut sauce, combine, and leave the hob alone for an hour. The difficulty sits in calibrating the sourness at the end, pomegranate syrup varies in acidity by brand, so you taste and adjust.

Bone-in thighs give the better result here because they hold up to the long simmer without drying. Boneless thighs work as a substitute if you want easier eating. Serve with steamed basmati rice and nothing more.
What you need

You need a wide, heavy casserole with a lid, a 26 cm cast-iron or enamelled pot works well. A food processor makes short work of grinding the walnuts; a blender or a very sharp knife will do the same more slowly.
Method
Grind the walnuts

Toast {{walnuts}} of walnuts in a dry pan over a medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring often, until they smell nutty. Leave to cool, then blitz in a food processor until very finely ground, the texture of coarse sand. Do not over-process or the oils will release and you end up with walnut butter rather than a dry powder.
Brown the chicken

Season {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs all over with salt, pepper, and {{turmeric}} of turmeric. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a heavy casserole over a medium-high heat. Brown the thighs in two batches, skin side down first, for 4 to 5 minutes per side. The skin should be deep golden. Set aside.
Cook the onion and build the sauce

In the same pot, soften {{onion}} large onion over a low-medium heat for 12 to 15 minutes until golden and sweet. Add {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper; stir for 1 minute. Add the ground walnuts and stir to combine. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and {{pomegranate-molasses}} of pomegranate syrup. Stir well, the sauce will look dark and grainy at this stage.
Slow-cook the stew

Return the browned chicken to the pot, skin side up. The sauce should come halfway up the thighs; add a splash more water if needed. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with the lid slightly ajar, and cook on the lowest heat for 60 to 70 minutes. The sauce will darken steadily. Stir every 20 minutes and check the liquid level. It should remain thick but pourable, not sticky.
Taste and adjust

When the sauce is dark brown and the chicken is completely tender, taste for seasoning. Pomegranate syrup varies in strength, if the sauce is too sour, add a small pinch of sugar and stir it in. If it lacks depth, add another small splash of pomegranate syrup and cook for 5 more minutes. Season with salt. The finished sauce should be richly savoury with a clear fruity sourness underneath.

items.fix: Push the finished stew through a coarse sieve or blitz briefly with a stick blender. The sauce will smooth out without losing character.
items.cause: The walnuts were not ground finely enough.

items.fix: Add a small pinch of sugar, starting with half a teaspoon; stir in, and simmer for 5 minutes. Taste again. A tiny splash of water can also dilute the sharpness without sweetening.
items.cause: Pomegranate syrup acidity varies significantly between brands.

items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice, stir in, and taste. A small pinch of salt also brings sharpness forward.
items.cause: Some pomegranate syrup brands are very sweet.

items.fix: Add a splash of warm water or stock and stir vigorously over a low heat until the sauce loosens. It should coat the back of a spoon, not set like jam.
items.cause: The heat was too high or the lid was fully on, reducing too fast.
items.symptom: Sauce has thickened to a paste

items.fix: Bone-in thighs are the right cut for this dish. If using boneless, check at 45 minutes rather than 70.
items.cause: Boneless or breast pieces dry out faster than bone-in thighs.

content.text: Variations

Duck legs work in place of chicken and give an even richer result, increase the cooking time to 90 minutes. A vegetarian version uses cubes of butternut squash; reduce the stock to 150 ml and cook for 40 minutes. Some cooks add a pinch of saffron dissolved in warm water at the end of cooking for fragrance.
Make ahead and freezing

Khoresh fesenjan improves by a day in the fridge. Cool fully, refrigerate, and reheat gently on the hob with a splash of water. It freezes for up to 3 months: the walnut sauce holds its texture well, though the skin softens on freezing. Remove the skin before freezing if you want the best result on reheating.
Where this dish lives

Fesenjan comes from the Caspian coast, from Gilan and Mazandaran. Walnut trees grow thick there and pomegranates are a harvest-season crop. The dish is a centrepiece at weddings and at Nowruz, the Iranian New Year, served alongside herb rice. It came to the British Iranian community largely unchanged, and is now made in London kitchens with the same ingredients as Tehran ones. The sauce colour is your guide: when it turns a dark walnut brown, the stew is ready.
